title: Virgin Islands Visa Waiver Act of 2025
short_title: Virgin Islands Visa Waiver Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill lets people from certain countries visit the US Virgin Islands for up to 45 days without getting a visa first. The Department of Homeland Security gets to decide which countries can be part of this program. They will only allow countries that do not pose a safety or security threat to the United States. This is similar to programs that already exist for Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Virgin Islands Visa Waiver Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Addition of Virgin Islands Visa Waiver to Guam and Northern Mariana Islands Visa Waiver
sections.text: This section changes existing immigration law to create a new visa waiver program for the Virgin Islands. The Secretary of Homeland Security can allow visitors from certain countries to enter the Virgin Islands without a visa for up to forty five days. This works the same way as the existing programs for Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands. The Secretary must first check with the Secretary of the Interior, the Secretary of State, and the Governor of the Virgin Islands. They must make sure the Virgin Islands has good systems to track who arrives and leaves. They also must be sure that allowing visitors without visas will not harm the safety or security of the United States or its territories. People who want to use this program must give up some legal rights. They cannot appeal if immigration officers decide they cannot enter the Virgin Islands. They also cannot fight removal from the country except in very specific cases involving asylum or protection from torture. The Secretary of Homeland Security will create rules for this program working with other government officials. These rules will list which countries can participate in the program. The rules may also require people from some countries to post bonds or pay money as security if they might be more likely to stay illegally. The Secretary will consider many factors when deciding which countries can participate. These include how well countries report lost or stolen passports, how often they help send people back to their home countries, and how often their citizens get rejected for regular visitor visas. The Secretary will watch how well the program works. If too many people from a particular country stay illegally or try to enter other parts of the United States illegally, the Secretary can stop allowing people from that country to use the program. The Secretary can also suspend the whole program at any time if needed. The governors of these territories can ask to add new countries to the program. The Secretary can approve these requests after consulting with other officials and may create special requirements for specific countries. Within one year of this law being passed, the Secretary must create the rules needed to start the Virgin Islands program. The rules must include a list of Caribbean Community member countries that can participate, unless including a specific country would threaten United States security or increase visa fraud. The law also makes small changes to other parts of immigration law to include references to the Virgin Islands alongside Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands. Finally, the Secretary must create a fee that people pay when using this program. The fee must cover all costs of running the program.
